subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 3. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, fold, cut and share alongside the child, using the simple scripts and hands-on activities set out below. The child's own written work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Understand that a half is one of two equal parts of a whole.
Recognise when a shape or object is divided into halves.
Divide simple shapes into halves by drawing a line.
Tell the difference between equal and unequal parts.
Know that two halves make a whole.
Complete the other half of a shape to make a whole.
Find and use halves in everyday life, such as sharing food fairly.
Paper shapes to fold and cut: circles, squares, rectangles and triangles (draw and cut a few from scrap paper).
Safety scissors (used with adult help) and a pencil or crayon.

concepts.title: A Whole and Its Parts
concepts.explanation: Before a child can understand a half, they need the words whole and part. Start with something complete — a whole anjero, a whole mango, a whole biscuit — and name it clearly as ‘the whole’. Then break or cut it and talk about the parts you have made. Keep the language simple and repeat it often across the week. How to teach it: Hold up a whole anjero. Say, ‘This is the whole anjero.’ Now tear it into two pieces and say, ‘Now I have two parts.’ At this first stage do not worry whether the parts are equal — the aim…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Look — this is a whole date. One whole date.” You: “Now I break it. How many parts do I have now?” Child: “Two!” You: “Yes. Two parts. Soon we will make the two parts fair and equal.”

concepts.title: Equal Parts vs Unequal Parts
concepts.explanation: This is the single most important idea in the unit: a half is not just any piece — it must be one of two equal parts. Young children often think that any two pieces are ‘halves’. The way in is fair sharing, something every child feels strongly about. If one child gets a big piece and the other a small piece, that is not fair, and it is not halves. How to teach it: Break a date or biscuit into one big piece and one small piece. Offer the child the small piece and ask, ‘Is this fair? Do we both have the same?’ The…
concepts.example: A powerful check for ‘equal’ is to fold and match. Fold a paper shape so the two parts sit exactly on top of each other; if the edges match perfectly, the parts are equal and you have made halves. If the edges do not…

concepts.title: Making Halves by Folding and Drawing
concepts.explanation: Once a child can spot equal parts, they are ready to make halves themselves. Folding comes first because the paper does the ‘measuring’ for them — when the edges match, the fold line shows a true half. Drawing a line comes next, and is harder, so be patient with wobbly lines. How to teach it: Give the child a paper circle. Ask them to fold it so ‘the two sides match exactly’. Open it up and trace the fold line together — ‘this line splits the circle into two halves’. Repeat with a square and a rectangle. Then move…
concepts.example: An important discovery for children is that a shape can be halved in more than one way. A square can be folded top-to-bottom, side-to-side, or corner-to-corner — and every one of those gives two equal halves. Show two…

concepts.title: Halves Can Be Different Shapes but Equal Size
concepts.explanation: Here is a subtle idea worth handling gently. When you fold a square corner to corner, the two halves are triangles — they look different from the halves you get folding straight across, which are rectangles. Yet all of these are still true halves, because each is one of two equal parts. The key message: halves must be equal in size, but they do not have to be the same shape as some other way of halving. How to teach it: Fold one square straight across and one square corner to corner. Lay the two triangles on top…
concepts.example: Here is a subtle idea worth handling gently. When you fold a square corner to corner, the two halves are triangles — they look different from the halves you get folding straight across, which are rectangles. Yet all of…

concepts.title: Two Halves Make a Whole
concepts.explanation: The final idea completes the circle: if you put the two halves back together, you get the whole again. This is what makes a half a half — two of them rebuild the original. It also quietly plants the seed that fractions are parts that add back up to one whole, an idea the child will meet properly in later years. How to teach it: After cutting a paper shape into two halves, ask the child to slide them back together to remake the whole. Say the words as they do it: ‘two halves… make one whole’. Play matching games…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “You cut the circle into two halves. Now push them back together.” Child: (slides the pieces together) You: “Look! Two halves make one whole circle again. Two halves make a whole.”

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Here is a subtle idea worth handling gently. When you fold a square corner to corner, the two halves are triangles — they look different from the halves you get folding straight across, which are rectangles. Yet all of these are still true halves, because each is one of two equal parts. The key message: halves must be equal in size, but they do not have to be the same shape as some other way of halving. How to teach it: Fold one square straight across and one square corner to corner. Lay the two triangles on top…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Two Halves Make a Whole.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The final idea completes the circle: if you put the two halves back together, you get the whole again. This is what makes a half a half — two of them rebuild the original. It also quietly plants the seed that fractions are parts that add back up to one whole, an idea the child will meet properly in later years. How to teach it: After cutting a paper shape into two halves, ask the child to slide them back together to remake the whole. Say the words as they do it: ‘two halves… make one whole’. Play matching games…

questions.question: What does “Whole” mean?
options: The complete thing before it is split
One of two equal parts of a whole
The same size as each other
questions.answer: The complete thing before it is split
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Whole means The complete thing before it is split.

questions.question: What does “Half” mean?
options: One of two equal parts of a whole
The same size as each other
questions.answer: One of two equal parts of a whole
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Half means One of two equal parts of a whole.

questions.question: What does “Equal” mean?
options: The same size as each other
Give out fairly, the same to each
questions.answer: The same size as each other
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Equal means The same size as each other.

questions.question: What does “Unequal” mean?
options: Give out fairly, the same to each
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Unequal means Not the same size.

questions.question: What does “Part” mean?
options: Give out fairly, the same to each
The complete thing before it is split
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Part means A piece of the whole.

selfAssessment: I can understand that a half is one of two equal parts of a whole.
I can recognise when a shape or object is divided into halves.
I can divide simple shapes into halves by drawing a line.
I can tell the difference between equal and unequal parts.
I can know that two halves make a whole.
I can complete the other half of a shape to make a whole.
I can find and use halves in everyday life, such as sharing food fairly.
I can required Materials (all low-cost / household)
